content:
# Exodus Chapter 40 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 112–113; Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 83–84 | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_040_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_040_page_002.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_040_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_040_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

### YHWH's Instructions to Set Up the Tabernacle (vv. 1–8)

**1** Then YHWH spoke to Moses: **2** "On the first day of the first month, you are to set up the tabernacle — the tent of meeting. **3** Place the ark of the covenant law inside it and screen off the ark with the curtain. **4** Bring in the table and arrange everything on it in order. Then bring in the lampstand and set up its lamps. **5** Place the gold altar for incense in front of the ark of the covenant law, and hang the screen at the entrance to the tabernacle.

**6** "Place the altar of burnt offering in front of the entrance to the tabernacle — the tent of meeting. **7** Set the basin between the tent of meeting and the altar, and put water in it. **8** Set up the courtyard all around and hang the screen at the courtyard entrance.

---

### Instructions to Anoint and Consecrate (vv. 9–16)

**9** "Then take the anointing oil and anoint the tabernacle and everything in it; consecrate it along with all its furnishings, and it will be holy. **10** Anoint the altar of burnt offering and all its utensils; consecrate the altar, and it will become most holy. **11** Anoint the basin and its stand, and consecrate them.

**12** "Then bring Aaron and his sons to the entrance of the tent of meeting and wash them with water. **13** Dress Aaron in the sacred garments, anoint him, and consecrate him so that he may serve me as priest. **14** Bring his sons forward and dress them in tunics. **15** Anoint them just as you anointed their father, so that they may serve me as priests. Their anointing will establish a permanent priesthood for them throughout every generation." **16** Moses did all of this — he did everything exactly as YHWH had commanded him.

---

### Moses Sets Up the Tabernacle (vv. 17–19)

**17** So on the first day of the first month, in the second year, the tabernacle was set up. **18** Moses erected the tabernacle: he laid its bases in place, set up its frames, inserted the crossbars, and raised the posts. **19** Then he spread the tent over the tabernacle and placed the outer covering over the tent — exactly as YHWH had commanded Moses.

---

### The Ark and the Curtain (vv. 20–21)

**20** He took the covenant law and placed it inside the ark, inserted the poles through the rings of the ark, and set the mercy seat on top of it. **21** Then he brought the ark into the tabernacle and hung the shielding curtain to screen off the ark of the covenant law — exactly as YHWH had commanded Moses.

---

### The Table and the Lampstand (vv. 22–25)

**22** He placed the table in the tent of meeting, on the north side of the tabernacle, outside the curtain. **23** He arranged the bread on it in the presence of YHWH — exactly as YHWH had commanded Moses.

**24** He placed the lampstand in the tent of meeting, opposite the table, on the south side of the tabernacle. **25** He set up the lamps in the presence of YHWH — exactly as YHWH had commanded Moses.

---

### The Gold Altar and the Incense (vv. 26–28)

**26** He placed the gold altar in the tent of meeting, in front of the curtain. **27** He burned fragrant incense on it — exactly as YHWH had commanded Moses. **28** Then he hung the screen at the entrance to the tabernacle.

---

### The Altar of Burnt Offering and the Basin (vv. 29–30)

**29** He placed the altar of burnt offering at the entrance to the tabernacle — the tent of meeting — and offered the burnt offering and the grain offering on it — exactly as YHWH had commanded Moses.

**30** He set the basin between the tent of meeting and the altar, and put water in it for washing. **31** Moses, Aaron, and his sons washed their hands and feet from it. **32** Whenever they entered the tent of meeting or approached the altar, they washed — exactly as YHWH had commanded Moses.

---

### The Courtyard Completed (vv. 33)

**33** Then he set up the courtyard around the tabernacle and the altar, and hung the screen at the courtyard entrance. So Moses finished the work.

---

### The Glory of YHWH Fills the Tabernacle (vv. 34–38)

**34** Then the cloud covered the tent of meeting, and the glory of YHWH filled the tabernacle. **35** Moses was unable to enter the tent of meeting because the cloud had settled on it and the glory of YHWH filled the tabernacle.

**36** Whenever the cloud lifted from above the tabernacle, the Sons of Israel would set out on their journey. **37** But if the cloud did not lift, they would not set out — they waited until the day it lifted. **38** For the cloud of YHWH rested on the tabernacle by day, and fire glowed within it by night, in full view of the entire house of Israel, throughout all their journeys.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### Verse 1–2 — The date and the divine command
The instruction to erect the tabernacle on בַּיּוֹם הָרִאשׁוֹן לַחֹדֶשׁ הָרִאשׁוֹן (bayyom harishon lachodesh harishon, "on the first day of the first month") — that is, the first of Nisan — is theologically weighted. This is nearly the anniversary of the exodus itself (which began on the fifteenth of Nisan), and it marks the beginning of a new ritual calendar. The tabernacle is set up at the start of a new year, a new era — the cosmic symbolism of creation's first day echoes through this dating. The verb תָּקִים (taqim, "you shall raise up/erect") uses the Hiphil of קום (qum, "to rise"), conveying the sense of causing the tabernacle to stand — bringing it from components into living, functioning wholeness. The LXX renders this as στήσεις τὴν σκηνήν (steseis ten skenen, "you shall set up the tent"), which Vaticanus on p. 83 preserves clearly.

### Verse 3 — Screening off the ark
The instruction וְסַכֹּתָ עַל־הָאָרֹן אֶת־הַפָּרֹכֶת (vesakota al-ha'aron et-happarokhet, "and you shall screen over the ark with the curtain") uses the verb סכך (sakhakh, "to cover, screen, overshadow"), the same root behind the word סֻכָּה (sukkah, "booth/shelter") — the temporary dwelling of the Feast of Tabernacles. The פָּרֹכֶת (parokhet, "curtain/veil") separated the Holy of Holies from the Holy Place, shielding the ark and the divine presence from direct approach. This is the same curtain that, according to the New Testament (Matt. 27:51), was torn at the crucifixion. The dynamic rendering "screen off" captures the protective and separating function. The LXX uses σκεπάσεις (skepaseis, "you shall shelter/cover"), emphasizing protection.

### Verses 9–11 — Anointing and degrees of holiness
The anointing oil (שֶׁמֶן הַמִּשְׁחָה, shemen hammishchah) transforms ordinary objects into sacred ones. A key distinction emerges: the tabernacle and its furnishings become קֹדֶשׁ (qodesh, "holy") through anointing (v. 9), but the altar of burnt offering becomes קֹדֶשׁ קָדָשִׁים (qodesh qadashim, "most holy" or "holy of holies") (v. 10). This graduated holiness — from holy to most holy — reflects the concentric sacred geography of the tabernacle complex: the courtyard is holy, the Holy Place is holier, the Holy of Holies is holiest, and the altar, where blood meets fire in the act of atonement, participates in the highest degree of sanctity. The LXX renders קֹדֶשׁ קָדָשִׁים as ἅγιον τῶν ἁγίων (hagion ton hagion, "holy of holies"), which became the standard phrase in Christian theology.

### Verse 15 — A perpetual priesthood
The phrase לִכְהֻנַּת עוֹלָם לְדֹרֹתָם (likhunnat olam ledorotam, "for a perpetual/everlasting priesthood throughout their generations") establishes the Aaronic priesthood as a permanent institution. The word עוֹלָם (olam, "forever/perpetuity") does not necessarily mean infinite duration in Hebrew thought; it means "for the foreseeable future" or "for as long as the relevant age lasts." The dynamic rendering "permanent priesthood for them throughout every generation" captures this institutional permanence without implying metaphysical infinity. The LXX uses ἱερατεία αἰώνιος (hierateia aionios, "eternal priesthood"), from which the theological concept of αἰώνιος (aionios, "age-lasting") derives — the same adjective later applied to "eternal life" in the New Testament.

### Verse 16 — The hinge verse
The statement וַיַּעַשׂ מֹשֶׁה כְּכֹל אֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה אֹתוֹ כֵּן עָשָׂה (vayya'as Mosheh kekhol asher tsivvah YHWH oto ken asah, "And Moses did according to all that YHWH commanded him — so he did") is a transitional hinge. It summarizes Moses's obedience to all the instructions just given (vv. 1–15) and introduces the execution narrative that follows (vv. 17–33). The doubled verb construction — "he did... so he did" — is emphatic in Hebrew, stressing completeness and exactness. The dynamic rendering "Moses did all of this — he did everything exactly as YHWH had commanded him" preserves this emphasis. This verse structurally parallels Genesis 6:22 (Noah's obedience: "Noah did everything; just as God commanded him, so he did"), linking Moses to Noah as covenant-faithful figures who build according to divine blueprints.

### Verse 17 — Chronological anchor
The date formula בַּחֹדֶשׁ הָרִאשׁוֹן בַּשָּׁנָה הַשֵּׁנִית בְּאֶחָד לַחֹדֶשׁ (bachodesh harishon bashannah hashenit be'echad lachodesh, "in the first month of the second year, on the first of the month") places the tabernacle erection exactly one year after the exodus. The tabernacle construction, from command to completion, mirrors the creation week — and now the erection happens at the turning of the year, marking a new beginning. The second year counts from the departure from Egypt, making this date approximately 1 Nisan in the second year — the very anniversary of Israel's liberation. The LXX preserves τῷ μηνὶ τῷ πρώτῳ τῷ δευτέρῳ ἔτει (to meni to proto to deutero etei, "in the first month of the second year").

### Verse 20 — The covenant law placed in the ark
The הָעֵדֻת (ha'edut, "the testimony/covenant law") refers to the stone tablets of the Ten Commandments. The word עֵדוּת (edut) means "testimony" or "attestation" — it is the divine witness to the covenant relationship. Placing the tablets inside the ark was the foundational act: everything else in the tabernacle — the mercy seat above, the curtain before, the incense altar beyond, the entire structure — existed to house and honor these tablets, which embodied the covenant between YHWH and Israel. The כַּפֹּרֶת (kapporet, "mercy seat" or "atonement cover") placed on top is related to the verb כִּפֶּר (kipper, "to atone/cover"), making the ark's lid the place where atonement is enacted — where divine justice (the law inside) meets divine mercy (the cover above). The LXX renders כַּפֹּרֶת as ἱλαστήριον (hilasterion, "place of propitiation"), the same word Paul uses in Romans 3:25.

### Verses 22–25 — Table and lampstand: north and south
The table (שֻׁלְחָן, shulchan) was placed on the north side, the lampstand (מְנוֹרָה, menorah) on the south — facing each other across the Holy Place. The bread arranged on the table is the לֶחֶם הַפָּנִים (lechem happanim, literally "bread of the face/presence"), rendered dynamically as "the bread... in the presence of YHWH" to convey the theological meaning: this bread stood perpetually before God's face. The lampstand's lamps were set up לִפְנֵי יְהוָה (lifnei YHWH, "before YHWH / in YHWH's presence"), emphasizing that the light served not for human convenience but as an offering of light before the divine presence. The north-south placement created a symbolic geography: the table of sustenance and the lampstand of illumination flanking the path toward God's presence behind the curtain.

### Verse 29 — The offerings begin
This is the first time sacrifices are actually offered on the completed altar. Moses himself performs the inaugural עֹלָה (olah, "burnt offering") and מִנְחָה (minchah, "grain offering"), acting in a priestly capacity before Aaron's formal consecration (described in Leviticus 8). This brief note marks a momentous transition: the tabernacle moves from structure to functioning sanctuary, from architecture to worship. The LXX on Vaticanus p. 84 preserves the sequence of ὁλοκαυτώματα (holokautomata, "whole burnt offerings") and θυσίας (thysias, "sacrifices").

### Verses 30–32 — Washing as approach
The laver (כִּיּוֹר, kiyyor, "basin") and its ritual washing represent the principle that approaching the holy requires preparation and purification. The washing of hands and feet — the parts of the body that do work and make contact with the ground — symbolized the cleansing of action and direction. The text notes that washing occurred בְּבֹאָם אֶל־אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד (bevo'am el-ohel mo'ed, "when they entered the tent of meeting") and בְּקָרְבָתָם אֶל־הַמִּזְבֵּחַ (beqorvatam el-hammizbeach, "when they approached the altar") — marking two thresholds of holiness that required ritual preparation.

### Verse 33 — Moses finishes the work
The phrase וַיְכַל מֹשֶׁה אֶת־הַמְּלָאכָה (vayekhal Mosheh et-hammelakhah, "and Moses finished the work") is the most explicit creation parallel in the entire tabernacle narrative. The verb כָּלָה (kalah, "to finish/complete") and the noun מְלָאכָה (melakhah, "work/craft") are precisely the words used in Genesis 2:2 — וַיְכַל אֱלֹהִים... מְלַאכְתּוֹ (vayekhal Elohim... melakhto, "and God finished... his work"). Moses completing the tabernacle recapitulates God completing creation. The tabernacle is a microcosm — a small, portable cosmos where heaven and earth meet. The dynamic rendering "So Moses finished the work" lets this echo speak for itself. The LXX uses συνετέλεσεν Μωυσῆς τὰ ἔργα (synetelesen Moyses ta erga, "Moses completed the works").

### Verses 34–35 — The glory fills the tabernacle
This is the climax of the entire book of Exodus. The עָנָן (anan, "cloud") — the same cloud that led Israel from Egypt (13:21), that descended on Sinai (19:16), that spoke with Moses (33:9) — now comes to rest permanently on the tent of meeting. The כְּבוֹד יְהוָה (kevod YHWH, "glory of YHWH") filled (מָלֵא, male') the tabernacle. The verb מָלֵא conveys total saturation — the glory did not merely enter but permeated every space. The astonishing statement that Moses himself could not enter (v. 35) demonstrates the overwhelming intensity of the divine presence: even Moses, who spoke with God face to face (33:11), was shut out by the sheer weight of glory. This scene directly parallels 1 Kings 8:10–11, where Solomon's temple is likewise filled with the cloud and glory so that the priests cannot stand to minister. The tabernacle is validated — God has moved in. The LXX renders כְּבוֹד with δόξα (doxa, "glory"), the word that would become central to Christian theology, and the Vaticanus text on p. 84 preserves this passage with great clarity: ἐκάλυψεν ἡ νεφέλη τὴν σκηνήν (ekalypsen he nephele ten skenen, "the cloud covered the tent") and δόξης κυρίου ἐπλήσθη ἡ σκηνή (doxes kyriou eplesthe he skene, "the tent was filled with the glory of the Lord").

### Verses 36–38 — The pillar of cloud and fire
The final three verses of Exodus transform the tabernacle from a static sanctuary into a mobile command center. The cloud (עָנָן, anan) becomes the navigation system for Israel's wilderness journey: when it lifts (בְּהֵעָלוֹת, behe'alot, "when it was taken up"), they march; when it stays, they camp. This is guidance by presence — not by map or strategy but by the visible, tangible dwelling of God among His people. The closing image is magnificent: אֵשׁ תִּהְיֶה לַיְלָה בּוֹ (esh tihyeh lailah bo, "fire would be in it by night") — the cloud by day becomes luminous fire by night, visible לְעֵינֵי כָל־בֵּית יִשְׂרָאֵל (le'einei khol-beit Yisra'el, "before the eyes of the entire house of Israel"). The phrase בְּכָל־מַסְעֵיהֶם (bekhol-mas'eihem, "in all their journeys") is the final word of Exodus, pointing forward — the story does not end here but launches into the wilderness narrative of Leviticus and Numbers. Exodus began with Israel enslaved and crying out; it ends with Israel free, God dwelling among them, and the cloud of glory leading them into the future. The LXX renders the fire image as πῦρ ἦν ἐπ᾽ αὐτῆς νυκτός (pyr en ep' autes nyktos, "fire was upon it by night"), and the Vaticanus text concludes the book with ἐναντίον παντὸς Ισραηλ (enantion pantos Israel, "before all Israel"), closing Exodus with this panoramic image of the entire community witnessing God's abiding presence.

### Vaticanus Divergences
The LXX in Codex Vaticanus (pp. 83–84) presents Exodus 40 with several notable features compared to the MT. (1) The chapter heading visible at the top of p. 83 (column heading area) marks this section, though the LXX chapter numbering differs from the MT in places; the LXX treats some of this material under different chapter divisions. (2) The Vaticanus text preserves the full sequence of erection commands (vv. 1–15) and the execution narrative (vv. 17–33) but with characteristic Greek compression of some repetitive Hebrew phrases — where the MT repeats כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה אֶת־מֹשֶׁה (ka'asher tsivvah YHWH et-Mosheh) eight times in vv. 19–33, the LXX preserves each instance as ὃν τρόπον συνέταξεν κύριος τῷ Μωυσῇ (hon tropon synetaxen kyrios to Mouyse), maintaining the full liturgical repetition. (3) The climactic vv. 34–38 are preserved with strong agreement between MT and LXX; this is theologically expected, as the theophany narrative was considered too sacred to compress or rearrange. (4) The LXX adds no significant material beyond the MT in this chapter — unlike some earlier chapters where the Greek tradition includes expansions. (5) The phrase כָל־בֵּית יִשְׂרָאֵל ("the entire house of Israel") in v. 38, the final phrase of Exodus, is rendered as παντὸς Ισραηλ (pantos Israel, "all Israel") in the LXX — a slight compression that drops the "house" metaphor but preserves the comprehensive scope: every person, the whole people, witnessed the glory. (6) The Vaticanus scribal hand on p. 84 shows the final lines of Exodus flowing into the beginning of Leviticus with minimal spacing, reflecting the ancient understanding of the Torah as a continuous narrative rather than five separate books.
